title: Tell Me What You Want
subtitle: Prompting as delegation, not coding.
lesson: How to write specific, context-rich prompts that produce usable work output.
emotionalBeat: First tiny win: that took eight minutes?
oneLineDescription: The one in which she types something vague into ChatGPT, gets back garbage, and discovers the problem was never the technology. It was the ask.
episodeDescription: The one in which she types something vague into ChatGPT, gets back the world's most useless paragraph, channels David Rose demanding very specific things, tries again with actual context, and watches AI produce something she would send to a VP.
previously: She opened all three tools, typed the same thing into each, rated them on the butterfly clip scale, picked her favourite, and survived entirely.
tryOn: Give AI the same real task twice: once vaguely, then once with audience, context, tone, length, and what not to include. Compare the outputs.
mainCharacterEnergy: I gave it context and it gave me back something I would actually use.
rememberLine: The Spice Girls didn't say "tell me what you vaguely want, what you sort of generally want." And neither should you. Be specific. Be bold. Be David Rose about it.
communityPrompt: Drop a vague prompt and let the room help make it specific.

reelHooks: The Spice Girls were right about prompting.
Stop asking AI to "make this better."
Be David Rose about your prompts.
Your prompt is not a wish. It is a brief.
social.carouselIdea: Bad prompt vs better prompt: how specificity changes the output.
social.storyPoll: Which feels harder: knowing what to ask AI, or trusting what it gives back?
social.captionAngle: Vague prompts create vague output. Specificity is not technical. It is delegation.
social.cta: Save this for your next messy email or stakeholder update.
